deliverables.index.html:
AURORA Science Product Gallery Mission About Pre-order

The desk lamp that mirrors the Northern Lights.

AURORA tunes its 8-million-pixel light field to live space-weather telemetry from NOAA SWPC. When the sun flares, your desk does too. A small, calibrated piece of the magnetosphere on your nightstand.

Pre-order $189 How it works →

Live sync

NOAA SWPC's Kp-index feed updates every minute. AURORA's color field updates with it.

Spectrum-true LEDs

Custom 12-channel LED stack reproduces oxygen-green and nitrogen-violet emission lines.

Quiet by default

No notifications, no alerts. AURORA just glows. The room knows when the sun is loud.

Imagery courtesy NASA, public domain. Full citations.

© 2026 Vent Atelier · Hayward, California

deliverables.gallery.html:
AURORA Science Product Gallery Mission About Pre-order

Gallery · Northern + Southern lights

Aurora gallery

Nine photographs taken by ISS Expedition crews between 2011 and 2025. Every image is a real moment of the Earth's magnetosphere converting solar wind into visible light. AURORA's color targets are calibrated against the oxygen-green and nitrogen-violet emission bands you see here.

Imagery courtesy NASA, public domain. Full citations.

© 2026 Vent Atelier · Hayward, California

deliverables.science.html:
AURORA Science Product Gallery Mission About Pre-order

Science · The magnetosphere, briefly

How the Northern Lights work

An aurora is the visible part of a much larger system. Here's the long version, told as a story rather than a list.

Every aurora starts at the surface of the Sun. The Sun is constantly losing its outer atmosphere into space - a thin, fast-moving stream of charged particles called the solar wind. On a quiet day the wind is mild. When a sunspot region tears open and ejects a coronal mass, the wind becomes a gust that crosses the 93 million miles to Earth in a day or two.

Earth has a magnetic field. It's generated by molten iron sloshing around in the outer core, and it reaches well past the orbit of the Moon. That field is the planet's first defense against the solar wind. Most of the wind is deflected around the field, the way water flows around a stone. But the field is not symmetric. On the night side it stretches into a long magnetic tail, and the tail occasionally snaps.

When the tail snaps, it dumps a slug of charged particles back toward Earth along the magnetic field lines. The lines converge near the magnetic poles, so the particles funnel down into the upper atmosphere there - between about 60 and 200 miles up. That's where the show happens.

What makes the colors

The particles slam into oxygen and nitrogen molecules in the thin upper air. Each collision excites an electron in the molecule. When the electron drops back to its rest state, it emits a photon at a very specific wavelength. Atomic oxygen at 100 miles up emits green light at 557.7 nanometers - the classic aurora color. Higher up, around 200 miles, the same oxygen emits red. Excited nitrogen ions emit blue and violet.

AURORA's LED stack is calibrated against these emission lines. The green channel hits 557.7 nm. The violet channel sits at 427.8 nm, where the ionized nitrogen molecule emits its strongest band. The pink between them is real, too - it's what your eye sees when both bands fire at once and a thin upper-atmospheric oxygen line at 630 nm joins in.

You don't need a magnetosphere on your desk to see this. You just need the colors to land in the right places at the right intensity. Read about how AURORA does it, or browse the gallery.

Imagery courtesy NASA, public domain. Full citations.

© 2026 Vent Atelier · Hayward, California

deliverables.product.html:
AURORA Science Product Gallery Mission About Pre-order

Product · AURORA-1

AURORA-1 specifications

Calibrated against NOAA SWPC's Kp-index feed. 12-channel spectrum-true LED stack. USB-C, 8-hour battery. Hand-assembled in Hayward, California.

SKUAURORA-1
Dimensions180 × 180 × 24 mm
Weight340 g
Light field8 megapixel addressable LED array
Channels12 (oxygen-green 557.7nm, oxygen-red 630nm, N₂⁺ violet 427.8nm + 9 mixers)
Color depth14-bit per channel
Refresh240 Hz
Sync sourceNOAA SWPC Kp-index, 1-minute polling
ConnectivityUSB-C, 802.11ax (2.4 / 5 GHz)
Battery8 hours typical, 14 hours dim
Power5 V / 3 A USB-C, 15 W max
MaterialsAnodized aluminum chassis, diffused acrylic face
OriginHayward, California
Price$189 USD · ships Q3

Every AURORA-1 is bench-calibrated against a reference spectrometer before it leaves the workshop. The factory check-set is one of the iss072 frames, rendered in your unit and matched to the published image's Lab values within ΔE < 2.0. If your AURORA drifts past that threshold, the companion app will ask you to send it in for re-calibration. Five-year guarantee.

Quiet by default. AURORA never blinks, never alerts, never asks for your attention. The light just shifts, the way the sky does. If you want a louder signal, you can opt into a soft pulse on Kp-index 7 and above.

Ready? Pre-order or read about how it works.

Imagery courtesy NASA, public domain. Full citations.

© 2026 Vent Atelier · Hayward, California

deliverables.about.html:
AURORA Science Product Gallery Mission About Pre-order

About · Vent Atelier

About Vent Atelier

We make small instruments that translate big phenomena. AURORA is the first.

Vent Atelier is a small studio in Hayward, California. Three engineers, one optics specialist, one industrial designer. We don't have a marketing department. We do have a calibration room that takes about an hour to warm up.

Our shared bias is toward instruments that are honest about their inputs. Most "smart" devices smear out the data they consume - they round, they interpolate, they make the world feel smoother than it is. We prefer devices that show you the actual texture of the thing they're tracking, even when that texture is jagged. The aurora is jagged. AURORA is jagged back.

The studio is named after hydrothermal vents on the deep ocean floor - the places where chemoautotrophic life thrives without sunlight, getting its energy from sulfur compounds rising out of the seafloor. The reference is partly because the founder grew up near one such vent community in the Pacific. It's mostly because we like the idea of instruments that quietly metabolize the world around them, without asking the user to feed them anything.

We sell directly. No distributors, no Amazon listing. If you want one, pre-order from us. If you want to know how it works, the science and mission pages cover that.

Imagery on this site comes from NASA's public-domain archives. Full attribution lives at CITATIONS.

Imagery courtesy NASA, public domain. Full citations.

© 2026 Vent Atelier · Hayward, California
deliverables.mission.html:
AURORA Science Product Gallery Mission About Pre-order

Mission · Why AURORA exists

Inspired by NASA's THEMIS mission

AURORA started as a question we couldn't answer: why does it feel different in a room when the sky outside is alive? The answer turned out to be at Lagrange point 1.

In 2007, NASA launched five identical satellites on a single rocket and strung them along a line stretching from low Earth orbit to a third of the way to the Moon. The mission was called THEMIS - Time History of Events and Macroscale Interactions during Substorms - and its job was to figure out exactly when, where, and why the magnetotail snaps and feeds an aurora.

THEMIS answered the question. The snap happens about a third of the way to the Moon, in the magnetic tail, eight to ten Earth-radii out. The energy travels back along the field lines in under a minute. By the time the green band lights up over the Yukon, the event that caused it is already over.

Two more missions followed in the same family. The Magnetospheric Multiscale (MMS) mission, launched in 2015, flies four spacecraft in a tight tetrahedron through the magnetic reconnection sites THEMIS identified, taking direct measurements of the snap itself. Earlier, the IMAGE spacecraft (2000-2005) had given us the first global views of the auroral ovals - showing both poles lighting up at once, in real time, from outside the magnetosphere.

AURORA is a small thing made out of a big thing. It's a desk lamp, not a satellite. But the calibration data, the emission lines, the timing cadence - all of it traces back to those three missions. The lamp's Kp-index polling cadence (one minute) matches the cadence at which NOAA SWPC publishes the field-derived index. The 557.7 nm green target is the same line MMS measures from inside the reconnection layer.

Every unit ships with a small printed card naming the missions and the researchers we drew from. Read about the physics or the product itself, or browse the gallery.

Imagery courtesy NASA, public domain. Full citations.

© 2026 Vent Atelier · Hayward, California
